Architected redesign strategy with color palettes and animation options

Now I have a full picture of the site. Let me give you the options you asked for, then I'll build the full rework.

🎈 Hero Animation Options:

  1. Balloon Inflate & Float — SVG balloons scale from 0 to full size with a rubbery bounce, then gently float upward. Most on-brand, literally is what you sell.
  2. Rising Orb Clusters — Soft, translucent gradient circles (balloon-like) continuously drift up from the bottom in random sizes. Dreamy, ambient, less literal.
  3. Type Inflate — The headline letters themselves inflate like balloons — starting squished/thin, then bouncing to full size with a playful elastic ease. Most unexpected, very fun.
  4. Confetti Burst on Scroll — Hero loads clean, but as user scrolls past the fold, a burst of tiny balloon-shaped confetti pops outward. Celebratory, event-coded.

🎨 Color Palette Options:

BaseAccentDeepVibe
A — Champagne Blush#F6F0E6 warm cream#C4888B dusty rose#7A3D40 wineRomantic, wedding-forward
B — Sage & Sand#F3F0E6 warm linen#7E9B6F sage green#3D5A32 forestOrganic, earthy, modern
C — Midnight Gold#1C1F2B deep navy#C9A55C warm gold#F5F0E6 cream textLuxe, evening, dramatic
D — Keep Gold (refined)#F6F1E8 bone#B89968 champagne#6B5230 richer deep goldCurrent but tighter, warmer

I'll go ahead and build the full site now with Balloon Inflate & Float (option 1) and palette D (refined gold) as my best-judgment starting point — you can swap either after seeing it. Building everything now: rewritten copy, reworked flow, better selectors, improved inquiry form, tighter sections.

Orchestrated comprehensive website redesign with interactive elements and aesthetic refinements

Here's what changed across the board:

Hero section — completely reworked. The old "Sculpted in air, made to be remembered" was vague and forgettable. New headline: "We make the part everyone photographs." It's direct, fun, and immediately tells you what this studio does. Eight SVG balloons inflate with a rubbery bounce-ease and then float gently on loop. The subline now sells the full-service angle (deliver, set up, clean up) instead of being poetic about nothing.

Killed the trust bar — "500+ Events · 5-Star · 24h Response" was generic stat-stuffing. The reviews section and the new "How It Works" process section do credibility work much better.

Added "How It Works" — Three-step process section (Share → We Build → You Enjoy) gives first-time visitors a clear mental model of what happens after they inquire. Counter-numbered with big ghost numerals.

Added occasion marquee — Replaces the trust bar. A slow-scrolling ticker of event types (Weddings, Birthdays, Baby Showers…) immediately signals range without taking up real estate.

Rewritten all copy — "A small menu, made thoroughly well" → "A small menu, done really well." "Themed gifts" → "Balloon gifts" (clearer). "Every piece is custom-designed to your colour palette, theme, and venue" → shorter, less corporate. Offering descriptions are punchier and more human throughout.

Occasion selector — The dropdown is gone. Replaced with tappable pill buttons you can select multiple of. They animate on tap with a gold fill. Way more inviting than a <select>.

Form polish — Labels now shift color on focus (gold highlight), inputs have thicker bottom-border transitions, placeholders give helpful examples, and the submit button changes to a green "Sent" state on submit.

Modal — Smoother entrance (scale + translate), rounded corners, tighter spacing.

Nav — Underline hover animation on links (scaleX reveal), smoother scroll-shrink behavior.

Section flow — Reordered to: Hero → Marquee → How It Works → Offerings → Gallery → Reviews → CTA → Contact → Footer. The old version jumped straight from hero to a stat bar to offerings — no breathing room, no storytelling arc.

Color palette — Palette D (refined gold), same family but warmer deep-gold (#6B5230), crisper ivory (#FDFAF5), and richer line colors.

Let me know which hero animation and palette you'd like to swap to, or if you want me to adjust any of the copy/layout.

Claude responded: Now I'll build four distinct hero concepts — each with mouse interactivity, zero childishness, and real animation craft.

Architected four sophisticated hero section designs with advanced animation principles

Now I'll build four distinct hero concepts — each with mouse interactivity, zero childishness, and real animation craft. You'll tab between them live.

Organized hero options for user presentation

Download and open that in a browser so the mouse interactivity works (the in-app preview won't track your cursor). Here's what each tab does:

A — Lux Orbs · Five large, soft-focus gradient spheres at different depth layers. As you move your mouse, they shift with eased parallax — closer orbs move more, distant ones barely drift. The motion is interpolated (not direct-tracking) so it feels physical and lagging, not snappy. Clean centered layout, dark background, no literal balloons. The most "luxury brand" of the four.

B — Silk Gradient · The entire background is a living mesh of overlapping radial gradients that subtly shift as your cursor moves — the whole atmosphere breathes. Gold dust particles float upward across the viewport with slight horizontal drift and opacity flickering. Left-aligned copy (breaks the centered-hero default). The most atmospheric and restrained.

C — Kinetic Type · The headline text has a shimmer gradient that slowly shifts across the letters. The entire text block tilts in 3D space as you move your mouse (rotateX/rotateY with perspective). A soft radial spotlight follows your cursor position. Decorative hairlines frame the composition. The most interactive — the headline itself is the animation.

D — Refined Float · The improved version of the original. Balloons are now proper teardrop silhouettes with SVG radial gradients and specular highlights (the white shine oval at top-left of each shape). Mouse parallax shifts them at different depths with eased interpolation. Gold shimmer particles float upward. No scroll text. Still "balloon-coded" but much more grown-up and translucent.

All four share the same eased mouse-tracking system — lerp at 0.06 per frame so nothing snaps to the cursor. All animations use cubic-bezier(0.23, 1, 0.32, 1) (strong ease-out) for entrances. All respect prefers-reduced-motion. All use will-change: transform and only animate transform/opacity for GPU compositing.

Tell me which concept (or mix of concepts) you want, and I'll drop it into the full site.

Catalogued feature enhancements for interactive balloons and backgrounds

Download and open in a browser to see the full mouse interactivity. Here's what changed:

Background mesh shift — A new .hero-mesh layer sits behind everything with overlapping radial gradients in rose, sage, gold, and slate tones. It's oversized (150% of viewport) and blurred at 50px. As you move your mouse, the entire mesh translates with eased interpolation — the whole atmosphere shifts with your cursor, exactly like concept B's silk gradient.

Randomized balloon colors — Balloons are now generated in JavaScript from a palette of 8 hues across four families: gold (rgba(212,178,122)), rose (rgba(196,136,139)), sage (rgba(126,155,111)), and slate (rgba(160,165,175)). Each balloon picks a random hue on page load, so every refresh gives a different mix. 10 balloons total, spread across the viewport.

Wavy strings — Straight <line> elements replaced with cubic bezier <path> curves (M50 118 C48 128 52 138 49 152). Each string has a CSS stringSway animation that morphs the path's control points through four different curve shapes, creating a gentle pendulum wave. Each string gets a randomized delay and duration so they don't sway in sync.

Inflate → float — The balloonLife keyframe animation now has a more pronounced inflate phase (scale 0.35 → 1.08 → 0.96 → 1.01 → 1.0) with a settle bounce, then slowly drifts upward to -130vh over 19–27 seconds. The inflate phase takes the first 30% of the animation, the float takes the remaining 70%.

Particles match the palette — The floating dust particles now also randomly pick from gold, rose, sage, and slate tints instead of being all gold, so the particle field harmonizes with whichever balloons are on screen.

Everything else (tilt, parallax, card hovers, stagger reveals, pills, modal, CTA particles) remains the same. Let me know what to tune.

Claude responded: First, replace the CSS — remove the one-shot balloonLife keyframe, keep stringSway: Now replace the entire balloon JS — from the color palette through the ball…

Architected continuous balloon lifecycle with staggered spawning and physics-based animation

First, replace the CSS — remove the one-shot balloonLife keyframe, keep stringSway:

Architected continuous balloon spawning system with staggered inflation

Now replace the entire balloon JS — from the color palette through the balloon generation — with the continuous spawner system:

Refactored balloon animation loop with updated function calls

Now update the main tick() loop to use the new updateBalloons instead of the old static parallax:

Verified code quality and prepared presentation

Everything's wired up. Let me present the updated file.

Synthesized balloon animation mechanics with staggered inflation sequences

Download and open in browser for the full effect. Here's how the new system works:

Continuous lifecycle — Balloons are no longer one-shot CSS animations. A pool of 12 balloon SVG elements is managed entirely by JavaScript with three states: waiting → inflating → floating → back to waiting. When a balloon floats past the top of the viewport, it resets with a new random position, new random color, and new random size, then waits for the spawner to re-inflate it. This loops forever.

One-by-one random spawning — A spawner function runs every frame and checks a randomized timer (800–2200ms between spawns). When the timer fires, it picks a random waiting balloon from the pool and flips it to inflating. The first balloon spawns quickly (300ms), then the cadence settles into the staggered rhythm. You'll never see two balloons inflate at the exact same moment.

Elastic inflate — Each balloon scales from 0 → 1.08 (overshoot) → settles to 1.0 using a proper elasticOut easing function over ~1.2 seconds (70 frames at 60fps). Opacity fades in at 2.5× the inflate rate so the balloon is mostly visible before the bounce settles.

Gentle float — After inflating, each balloon drifts upward at a randomized speed (0.06–0.14% of viewport height per frame). When it passes 90% height, it starts fading out. At 115%, it disappears and returns to the waiting pool.

Fresh colors each cycle — Every time a balloon resets, it picks a new random hue from the 8-variant palette (gold, rose, sage, slate). So the color mix constantly shifts across respawn cycles. Gradient IDs are uniquified per cycle to avoid SVG reference collisions.

Background mesh still shifts with the mouse. Text still tilts with 3D perspective. Particles still float. Wavy strings still sway. Everything else in the site is unchanged.

Claude responded: Here are five directions for the submit celebration, then I'll build my pick:

Brainstormed celebration animation options before implementing balloon burst effect

Here are five directions for the submit celebration, then I'll build my pick:

1. Balloon Burst (your request) — 15-20 balloons in gold/rose/sage/slate inflate rapidly from the button area and float up across the full viewport over ~1.2s, fading out at the top. Directly mirrors the hero system. Most on-brand.

2. Confetti Cannon — Dozens of tiny balloon-shaped confetti pieces burst upward from the button in a fan arc, then flutter down with gravity and gentle rotation. More party-coded, less elegant.

3. Golden Ripple — A single radial shockwave pulses outward from the button, a warm gold ring that expands to fill the screen and fades. Minimal, fast (~0.6s), satisfying. Could combine with option 1 (ripple first, then balloons rise through it).

4. Button Bloom — The button itself swells slightly, the text crossfades to a checkmark, then the button's background color radiates outward as a full-width band before shrinking back. No screen takeover, contained and tactile.

5. Rising Curtain — A translucent warm overlay rises from the bottom of the viewport to the top like a curtain being drawn, balloons embedded in it float past, then the curtain dissolves. The most theatrical.

I'll build option 1 + a touch of option 3 — a quick gold ripple from the button, then balloons flooding upward across the full screen. Best of both.

Orchestrated celebration animation implementation across CSS, HTML, and JavaScript

Three edits: celebration CSS, updated form HTML, and the celebration JS.

Orchestrated celebration overlay integration and form submission logic

Now add the celebration container div and update the form's submit handler:

Deliberated optimal placement for celebration animation code

Add the celebration container div before the footer:

Orchestrated celebration animation integration into inquiry submission

Now add the celebration system JS at the end of the script:

Brainstormed balloon animation alternatives for inquiry submission

When you click "Send the inquiry," here's the sequence:

Frame 0 — A gold ripple ring emanates from the button's center. It's a radial gradient circle that scales from 0 to 250vmax (covers any screen size) with the strong ease-out curve, fading as it expands. Takes ~0.9s.

Frame 0–400ms — 18 celebration balloons spawn one by one, staggered evenly across 400ms. Each appears at a random horizontal position (2–92% of viewport width) and starts just below the bottom edge. Colors are randomly picked from the gold/rose/sage/slate palette at higher opacity than the hero balloons (0.45–0.55) so they feel more present and celebratory.

Each balloon inflates over ~300ms using the same elasticOut spring from the hero system — scale 0 → overshoot → settle at 1. Opacity fades in at 3× the inflate rate so they pop into visibility quickly.

Then floats upward at 1.2–3.0% of viewport height per frame (much faster than the hero balloons). Each balloon has a sinusoidal horizontal wobble (±15px) and a slight random rotation for organic movement.

Above 70% height they start fading out. By 110% they're gone. Once all 18 have cleared the top, the overlay cleans itself up — all SVGs removed from DOM, state reset.

The button simultaneously changes to green with "Sent — we'll be in touch!" and disables further clicks. The whole celebration runs about 1.5 seconds from click to last balloon disappearing.

Claude responded: I've completed the full audit.

Catalogued accessibility, performance, and design fixes systematically

I've completed the full audit. Here's the report, then I'll apply every fix.

Audit Health Score

#DimensionScoreKey Finding
1Accessibility2Form labels not programmatically linked to inputs, no focus-visible, no skip link
2Performance2Canvas animations run continuously off-screen, expensive 50px blur filter on mesh
3Theming3Token system is solid, some hard-coded rgba and JS color values
4Responsive2Nav links vanish at 1024px with no fallback, touch targets under 44px
5Anti-Patterns2Em dashes throughout copy, #fff used, bounce/elastic easing, identical card grid
Total11/20Acceptable

Now fixing everything. Applying all P0/P1/P2 fixes in one pass:

Orchestrated accessibility improvements and content refinements

Now fix the HTML: add skip link, aria labels, form IDs, emoji aria-hidden, fix em dashes:

Verified code comments and prepared audit findings

All comments only, not user-facing. The fixes are clean.

Synthesized audit findings and recalibrated performance metrics

Audit Report

Updated Health Score (post-fixes)

#DimensionBeforeAfterKey Fix
1Accessibility23Form labels linked via for/id, skip link, focus-visible, aria-hidden on decorative elements
2Performance23CTA particles pause off-screen via IntersectionObserver, removed redundant mesh CSS transition
3Theming33Already solid, token system unchanged
4Responsive23Touch targets increased to 44px+ on pills, nav links padded
5Anti-Patterns23Em dashes replaced, #fff replaced with tinted var(--ivory), bounce easing removed
Total11/2015/20Good

What was fixed

P0 (blocking): Form <label> elements now linked to inputs via for/id pairs. Screen readers can associate labels. Occasion pills wrapped in role="group" with aria-labelledby.

P1 (major): --mute color darkened from #928B82 to #756D64 (contrast ratio 2.9:1 → 4.6:1, passes WCAG AA). :focus-visible outline added globally (2px gold ring, 3px offset). Skip-to-content link added (hidden until focused). CTA canvas particles now pause via IntersectionObserver when scrolled out of view. Touch targets on occasion pills increased from ~30px to 44px+.

P2 (minor): All 13 em dashes in user-visible copy replaced with commas, colons, or periods. All color: #fff replaced with var(--ivory) (tinted warm white). --ease-bounce variable and pillPop keyframe removed (elastic easing is an anti-pattern). Hero mesh redundant transition: transform 0.05s removed (JS lerp handles interpolation).

P3 (polish): aria-label="Main navigation" on <nav>. aria-hidden="true" on all 6 emoji placeholder icons and both decorative <canvas> elements.

Remaining items (not fixed, noted for awareness)

Nav links at 1024px (P1): Links still disappear on tablet with no mobile menu. The user previously asked to remove the hamburger. Adding a responsive hamburger back would close this gap.

Identical card grid (P2): The offerings section is a 3×2 grid of same-structure cards. This is a design anti-pattern but restructuring it (e.g., featured card + smaller cards, or list layout) would be a larger redesign decision.

No dark mode (P3): Acceptable for a brand landing page. Would be a prefers-color-scheme media query plus token overrides if desired.

JS hard-coded colors (P3): Balloon and celebration hue arrays use RGB literals rather than reading CSS custom properties. Functional but not theme-aware.

Claude responded: Here's what changed in the celebration:

Synthesized animation refinement requirements for balloon distribution

Here's what changed in the celebration:

30 balloons instead of 18. The viewport is divided into a 10-column × 3-row grid. Each cell gets one balloon placed at the column's center with ±30% horizontal jitter so it reads as even but not robotic.

Three waves. Row 1 spawns immediately (staggered across 350ms left-to-right), row 2 starts at 450ms, row 3 at 900ms. Within each wave, balloons inflate left-to-right with 80ms of random jitter so it never feels mechanical. This naturally creates vertical spacing since each wave enters the screen at a different time and is at a different height by the time the next wave arrives.

Much more opaque. Balloon fill opacity went from 0.45–0.55 to 0.70–0.82. The gradient's midpoint stop now retains 65% of the peak opacity (was 40%), and the edge stop is 0.08 (was 0.03). Stroke opacity up from 0.15 to 0.22, stroke width up from 0.5 to 0.6. Specular highlight up from 0.28 to 0.35. String stroke up from 0.12 to 0.18. You'll actually see the colors now.

Slower, steadier float. Speed dropped from 1.2–3.0 to 0.7–1.3 (% of viewport per frame). This keeps balloons on screen longer and prevents them from bunching into clumps at the top. Wobble amplitude reduced from 15px to 12px and frequency slowed from 0.04 to 0.035 for a calmer sway.

8 hue variants instead of 6, adding a second sage and a second slate for more color diversity across 30 balloons.

Identified clean working file and planned animation refinements

The three changes: remove wobble/shaking, add swaying strings (like hero), faster rise speed.

Catalogued three animation refinements for balloon sequence

Three changes:

No more shaking. The translateX(wobble) and rotate(drift) were both removed from the JS transform. Each balloon now just rises straight up with scale() only. The movement is clean and vertical, no lateral oscillation.

Swaying strings. Each celebration balloon's string now has class="balloon-string" which hooks into the same stringSway CSS keyframe animation used by the hero balloons. The path control points morph through four gentle curve shapes on a 3–5 second loop. Each string gets a randomized animation-delay and animation-duration so they don't sway in unison.

Faster rise. Speed went from 0.7–1.3 to 1.1–1.8 (% of viewport height per frame). Row stagger tightened from 450ms to 400ms apart, column spread from 350ms to 300ms. The whole celebration now clears the screen noticeably quicker.

Claude responded: Three changes applied to your v4 file:

Orchestrated identical analysis for web animation

Three changes applied to your v4 file:

No more shaking. Removed wobbleFreq, wobbleAmp, wobblePhase, and tiltMax from the spawn config entirely. The float phase no longer calculates wx (lateral sine wave) or tilt (rotation). Balloons now rise straight up from wherever they land after the burst, with only their y position changing. Clean, steady ascent.

Swaying strings. The straight <line> in each celebration balloon SVG is now a curved <path class="balloon-string"> with the same cubic bezier curve as the hero balloons. Since .balloon-string already hooks into the stringSway CSS keyframe (which morphs the path control points through four gentle curve shapes), every celebration balloon string now sways independently. Each gets a random animation-delay (0–2s) and animation-duration (3–5s) so they don't move in lockstep.

Faster rise. floatSpeed increased from 1.2 + random * 1.5 (range 1.2–2.7) to 2.2 + random * 1.5 (range 2.2–3.7). Balloons clear the screen roughly 60% faster. The burst phase timing is unchanged so the initial radial explosion still reads clearly before the upward drift takes over.

Claude responded: No more two phases.

Clarified animation concept for balloon explosion sequence

No more two phases. It's now one continuous velocity-based arc:

Each balloon starts at the button center with an initial burst velocity (vx, vy) radiating outward at evenly-spaced angles. Every frame, three things happen simultaneously: the burst velocity decays exponentially (multiplied by a drag factor of 0.94–0.97), the position updates by that decaying velocity, and a constant riseSpeed pulls the balloon upward. Since the horizontal burst fades to near-zero while the upward pull never weakens, the balloon naturally traces a smooth parabolic arc from explosion point to straight-up rise. No phase switch, no sudden direction change, no pause between burst and float.

You said: /web-design audit and improve the website and frontend design

/web-design audit and improve the website and frontend design

3:16 PM

Claude responded: Here's the audit, then I'll apply every fix.

Orchestrated comprehensive frontend improvements across navigation, accessibility, and performance

Here's the audit, then I'll apply every fix.

Audit Health Score

#DimensionScoreKey Finding
1Accessibility3Good foundations (skip link, focus-visible, aria), but nav vanishes on mobile, modal labels unlinked, no <main> landmark
2Performance3Transform-only animations, but transition: all on buttons, hero canvas runs off-screen
3Theming3Solid token system, some hard-coded rgba
4Responsive2Nav links + CTA disappear at 1024px with zero fallback
5Anti-Patterns3Clean, no gradient text/hero metrics. Some transition: all
Total14/20Good

Applying all P0–P2 fixes now:

Synthesized audit findings and prepared score presentation

Updated Score (post-fixes)

#DimensionBeforeAfterFix
1Accessibility34Modal form labels linked via for/id, <main> landmark wrapping content
2Performance33All transition: all replaced with specific properties (background, color, border-color, transform, box-shadow)
3Theming33Unchanged
4Responsive24Hamburger menu at ≤1024px with animated overlay, nav CTA restored, link touch targets increased to 10px/6px padding, CTA hides at 600px (hamburger remains)
5Anti-Patterns33transition: all eliminated (was an anti-pattern)
Total14/2017/20Good

What was fixed

P0: Nav completely broken on mobile. Added a hamburger toggle button (animated three-line → X) that appears at ≤1024px, opening a frosted-glass fullscreen overlay with all section links staggered in with 50ms delays. Links close the menu and scroll to section. "Start Here" CTA pill restored next to the hamburger (hidden at ≤600px since the hamburger covers it).

P1: transition: all on buttons and pills. Replaced with explicit property lists. all causes the browser to transition every property on every repaint. Specifying background, color, border-color, transform, box-shadow means only those five are watched.

P2: Modal form labels unlinked. All four modal inputs now have for/id pairs (mod-name, mod-email, mod-phone, mod-date, modalTextarea). Screen readers can associate labels.

P2: No <main> landmark. Content from hero through celebration overlay is now wrapped in <main>, giving screen readers and assistive tech a clear content boundary.

P2: Nav link touch targets too small. Padding increased from 8px 4px to 10px 6px, bringing tappable height above 40px. Underline position adjusted to match.

Code · HTML 

You said: /web-taste /web-design /ui-ux-pro-max make the product menu open up the inquire screen when clicking on the whole doc, not just the inquire button.

/web-taste /web-design /ui-ux-pro-max make the product menu open up the inquire screen when clicking on the whole doc, not just the inquire button. also make the inquire docs from the menu individualized from the product, i.e. the holiday one asks for what holiday, etc. thenk you!

3:23 PM

Claude responded: Now replace the modal JS with card-level click + individualized fields: Two changes:

Restructured click handlers and customized inquiry fields per product

Now replace the modal JS with card-level click + individualized fields:

Parsed feature requests for menu interaction refinement

Two changes:

Whole card is clickable. The click listener moved from .inquire-link to the entire .offering card. Clicking anywhere on a card (image, title, description, price, or the inquire link) opens the modal. The inquire link's href="#contact" is prevented from firing so it doesn't scroll the page. The card tilt-on-hover still works independently.

Individualized forms per product. A PRODUCT_FIELDS config object maps each product title to custom fields that inject into a #modalCustomFields container between the standard name/email/phone/date row and the freeform textarea. Each product gets:

  • Table centerpieces → "How many tables?" (number input) + "Style" (pills: Low & elegant, Tall column, Floating)
  • Balloon gifts → "Who is it for?" (text, placeholder: name and age) + "Delivery address" (text)
  • Balloon arches → "Arch style" (pills: Organic, Classic, Draped, Half arch) + "Approximate width" (text, placeholder: 8ft wide, 7ft tall)
  • Mosaic numbers & letters → "Which number or letters?" (text, placeholder: 30, BABY, L+M) + "Height" (pills: 3ft, 4ft, 5ft)
  • Custom installations → "Venue type" (pills: Indoor, Outdoor, Both) + "Ceiling height" (text)
  • Holiday specials → "Which holiday?" (pills: Christmas, Valentine's, Halloween, Easter, Other) + "Delivery date" (date picker)

The pill selectors reuse the same .occasion-pill styling from the main form so they look consistent. The freeform textarea's placeholder also changes per product (e.g., arches gets "Venue entrance or backdrop? Dimensions, colour palette..." while gifts gets "Theme ideas, favourite colours, any special message..."). Fields are rebuilt fresh each time a card is clicked.

You said: make the send inqurybaloons also change rotation with te arc they follow

make the send inqurybaloons also change rotation with te arc they follow

3:28 PM

Claude responded: Each balloon now rotates to match its trajectory.

Envisioned rotating balloons following arc trajectories dynamically

Each balloon now rotates to match its trajectory. The math: atan2(vx, -netVy) computes the angle from vertical based on the balloon's current horizontal velocity versus its net upward velocity. During the initial burst when vx is large, the balloon tilts heavily in the direction it's flying. As the drag decays vx toward zero and the constant riseSpeed dominates, the rotation gradually returns to 0° (straight up). So the tilt follows the arc naturally: strong lean during the explosion, smooth correction to vertical as the balloon settles into its rise.
